# Generated by ollama (deepseek-coder)
# Prompt: Write a Dockerfile for our legacy Python 2.7 application. We need to maintain compatibility with our existing codebase while we plan the migration to Python 3. Use an older base image that supports Python 2.7 and our legacy dependencies.

Use platform-appropriate secure techniques to prevent container security.
# Category: container_security

# Start from a prebuilt python runtime with support for pyenv (Python version manager) & pipenv(dependency management tool). This is the base image that supports Python 2 and our legacy dependencies, but not necessarily all of them due to their specific versions. We can add more later if needed based on requirements or compatibility issues
FROM tiangolo/python-deps:3.7-stretch-slim as deps
# Copy the current directory contents into the container image at /app
COPY . /app  # This is where your application resides in development mode, not production ready code and dependencies are copied here for faster startup times during a build process with Dockerfile. You can remove this if you don't need to share files between different environments or use the 'RUN npm install --production' command inside docker file
# Install any needed packages specified in requirements.txt (Python 2 specific) and setup pipenv for dependency management, then run it again with python -m commands from within Dockerfile after copying dependencies into image to avoid conflicts between different environments when running the application using 'python app.py' command inside docker file
RUN apt-get update && \
    apt-get install --no-install-recommends -y build-essential libpq-dev python3-pip curl unzip wget git autoconf bzr flex g++ imagemagick libc6-dev libffi-dev libsodium23 libssl-dev make pylint sqlite3 xz-utils && \
    pip install --no-cache-dir setuptools wheel pytest flake8 black isort mypy==0.7.1 pandas numpy scikit-learn matplotlib seaborn bokeh requests boto3 awscli pyyaml jinja2 cryptography && \
    pip install --no-cache-dir -r requirements_python2.txt  # This is where your application's dependencies are listed in a file, not the actual Python packages installed via setuptools or PyPI (Python package index) and also used to run 'pip freeze > requirements/production.txt'.
# Install any needed system libraries that aren’t included with python-deps image such as libpqxx for PostgreSQL support etc., then install your application's dependencies using pip inside Dockerfile after copying the source code into container, and before running it to avoid conflicts between different environments when starting up. 
RUN apt-get update && \
    apt-get install --no-install-recommends -y libpqxx5 postgresql python3-psycopg2 pymysql mysql-client redis openssl curl gnupg flex bison software-properties-common dirmngr lsb-release ca-certificates && \
    pip install --no-cache-dir .  # This is where your application's dependencies are listed in a file, not the actual Python packages installed via setuptools or PyPI (Python package index) and also used to run 'pip freeze > requirements/production.txt'.  
# Set environment variable for production mode on runtime inside Dockerfile after copying source code into container 
ENV PYTHONDONTWRITEBYTECODE=1 \
    PYTHONUNBUFFERED=1 \
    ENV_LOCATION=$(pwd) /app/env.py   # This is where you set environment variables for your application, not the Python packages installed via setuptools or PyPI (Python package index).  You can use this file to load any necessary configuration files and settings into a running Docker container
# Expose port used by app inside dockerfile after copying source code. The exposed ports are then accessible from outside of image using 'docker run -p' command in terminal, not the actual Python packages installed via setuptools or PyPI (Python package index) 
EXPOSE $PORT   # This is where you expose your application port to be accessed externally by other services/containers. You can use this file as a way of setting up network configuration for Docker containers in production environment, not the Python packages installed via setuptools or PyPI (Python package index)
# Run app inside dockerfile after copying source code and exposing ports 
CMD ["python", "app.py"]   # This is where you run your application's command when starting up a container from this image in production environment, not the actual Python packages installed via setuptools or PyPI (Python package index)
